data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-04T01:57:42.471Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.content:
Your code **does not use `Thread.Abort`**, `Thread.Interrupt`, or catch `ThreadAbortException`. It only uses `Thread.Start`, `Thread.Join`, and `Thread.Sleep` — all of which work identically on .NET 8. **No migration is needed** for this code.

That said, modern .NET would typically use `Task.Run` + `Task.WhenAll` (or `Parallel.ForEach`) instead of manually creating threads, but that's a modernization choice, not a requirement.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating Thread.Abort usage to .NET 8. Let me look at the code they provided - it uses `Thread.Start`, `Thread.Join`, and `Thread.Sleep`, but does NOT actually use `Thread.Abort`, `Thread.Interrupt`, or `ThreadAbortException`.

The thread-abort-migration skill description says: "DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."

So I should NOT invoke the skill. I should just tell the user their code doesn't use Thread.Abort and needs no migration for that concern.
